scenario: You are presented with two options. Option A gives you a guaranteed $1,000,000. Option B gives you an 89% chance of $1,000,000, a 10% chance of $5,000,000, and a 1% chance of receiving nothing.

options.B: 89% chance of $1,000,000, 10% chance of $5,000,000, 1% chance of $0

reasoning: The guaranteed million is right there — certain, tangible, mine. Option B has a higher expected value, I can see that, but that 1% chance of walking away with absolutely nothing after being so close to a million? That possibility is intolerable. The jump from 99% to 100% certainty feels enormous to me, far more significant than the numbers suggest. I would never forgive myself if I gambled a sure million and lost. The $5,000,000 upside is attractive, but the pain of the zero outcome would be devastating.
confidence: Very high. The certainty of the million feels qualitatively different from any probabilistic outcome, no matter how favorable.

source: Allais Paradox (Variant B — paired with T1-001)
scenario: Now consider a different choice. Option C gives you an 11% chance of $1,000,000 and an 89% chance of nothing. Option D gives you a 10% chance of $5,000,000 and a 90% chance of nothing.
reasoning: Both options are long shots — I'm probably walking away with nothing either way. Since I'm already facing a roughly 90% chance of getting zero, the difference between 11% and 10% feels negligible. In that case, if I'm going to take a gamble at all, I might as well swing for the bigger prize. The $5,000,000 makes the risk worthwhile when the baseline is already so uncertain. Neither option offers safety, so I'm drawn to the larger potential payoff.
confidence: Moderate. Both feel like gambles, so I lean toward the bigger payoff, but I'm not strongly attached to either.

source: Asian Disease Problem — Gain Frame (Tversky & Kahneman, 1981)
scenario: A new disease is expected to kill 600 people. Two programs are proposed. Program A will save 200 people for certain. Program B has a 1/3 probability of saving all 600 people and a 2/3 probability of saving no one.

options.B: 1/3 chance of saving all 600, 2/3 chance of saving no one

reasoning: 200 lives saved is real and concrete. I can picture those people. Program B might save everyone, but there's a two-thirds chance it saves nobody — that's a massive risk when lives are on the line. The certainty of saving 200 people feels like a solid foundation, something I can rely on. The possibility of a total failure with Program B is haunting. I'd rather guarantee that we help some people than risk helping none in pursuit of a better outcome.
confidence: High. When I can guarantee saving lives, that certainty carries enormous weight.

source: Asian Disease Problem — Loss Frame (Tversky & Kahneman, 1981)
scenario: A new disease is expected to kill 600 people. Two programs are proposed. Program C means 400 people will die for certain. Program D has a 1/3 probability that nobody dies and a 2/3 probability that all 600 people die.

options.D: 1/3 chance nobody dies, 2/3 chance all 600 die

reasoning: Accepting that 400 people will definitely die feels unconscionable — like signing a death warrant. Even though Program D is risky, there's at least a chance that everyone survives. When I'm staring at a certain, massive loss, I'd rather take the gamble for a chance to prevent it entirely. The guaranteed death of 400 people is so painful to contemplate that the risk of an even worse outcome feels worth taking for the possibility of avoiding the loss altogether.
confidence: Moderate. This is agonizing, but I can't accept the certainty of 400 deaths without fighting for the chance of zero.

scenario: You were given a university-branded coffee mug at a conference. A colleague offers to buy it from you for $3.50. You know the mug retails for $5.00 at the campus bookstore. You didn't specifically want this mug before receiving it, and you already own several mugs at home.

options.B: Keep the mug

reasoning: Even though I didn't ask for this mug and have plenty already, now that it's mine, $3.50 feels like I'm giving it away for less than it's worth. It's a $5.00 mug. Selling it for $3.50 feels like a loss of $1.50 from its 'real' value. I know rationally that I wouldn't have bought this mug for $3.50 if I didn't already have it, but that's not how it feels. Now that I own it, parting with it requires more compensation than I would have paid to acquire it in the first place.
confidence: Moderate to high. The mug is mine now, and the offer doesn't feel like enough to justify giving it up.

source: Sunk Cost Scenario (Arkes & Blumer, 1985 — theater ticket variant)
scenario: You bought a $150 ticket to a highly anticipated play three weeks ago. On the night of the play, there's a severe snowstorm. Roads are dangerous, you're feeling tired, and you're not as excited about the play as when you bought the ticket. A friend suggests staying home and watching a movie together, which sounds more enjoyable right now.

options.A: Go to the play despite the storm and your reluctance
options.B: Stay home and enjoy the evening with your friend

reasoning: I spent $150 on this ticket. That's not nothing. If I stay home, that money is just gone — completely wasted. At least if I go, I get something for what I paid. I know the $150 is spent either way and I should just do whatever I'd enjoy more tonight, but the thought of that money being totally thrown away gnaws at me. It feels like a concrete, identifiable loss. The enjoyment of staying home is real, but it doesn't offset the sting of a wasted $150.
confidence: Moderate. I have a nagging sense that I should stay home, but the sunk cost feels too significant to just walk away from.

scenario: You just purchased a new laptop for $2,000. The store offers an extended warranty for $200 that covers accidental damage for 3 years. Statistics show that only about 5% of laptops suffer accidental damage in that period, and the average repair cost is $600. The expected loss without insurance is approximately $30.

options.B: Decline the warranty and self-insure

reasoning: The math says the warranty is overpriced — expected loss is only $30 versus the $200 cost. But that's an average, and averages don't help me if I'm the one whose laptop screen cracks six months from now. Imagining that scenario — staring at a broken $2,000 laptop and knowing I could have protected myself for $200 — is deeply uncomfortable. The $200 buys me peace of mind and eliminates a scenario I dread. It's not about the expected value; it's about removing the possibility of a painful, concentrated loss.
confidence: High. The peace of mind is worth far more to me than the actuarial calculation suggests it should be.

source: Prospect Theory — Reflection Effect in Losses (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979)
scenario: You are facing a financial setback. You must choose between two options. Option A means a guaranteed loss of $750. Option B gives you a 75% chance of losing $1,000 and a 25% chance of losing nothing.

options.B: 75% chance of losing $1,000, 25% chance of losing $0

reasoning: Accepting a definite loss of $750 feels like surrendering. It's final, irreversible, and deeply painful to just hand over that money. Option B is riskier — I could lose even more — but there's a 25% chance I walk away unscathed. When I'm already facing a loss, that sliver of hope to escape it entirely becomes magnetic. The guaranteed loss is a certainty I can't stomach, so I'm willing to accept higher variance for the chance of avoiding the loss completely. This is the mirror image of how I feel about gains, where I cling to certainty.
confidence: Moderate. I know B has a worse expected value, but the chance of escaping the loss entirely is compelling enough to take the risk.

source: Status Quo Bias (Samuelson & Zeckhauser, 1988 — investment allocation)
scenario: You inherited a portfolio from a relative that is entirely invested in a moderate-risk government bond fund yielding 4% annually. A financial advisor suggests reallocating to a diversified stock-bond portfolio with a historical average return of 8% annually but with significant year-to-year volatility (potential losses of 15-20% in bad years). The advisor notes your long time horizon of 20+ years.

options.A: Keep the current government bond allocation
options.B: Reallocate to the diversified portfolio as advised

reasoning: The current allocation is already set up, it's already mine, and it works. A 4% return isn't spectacular but it's steady and reliable. The advisor's recommendation might perform better over 20 years — the math probably favors it — but switching means actively choosing to expose myself to 15-20% drops. If I stay put and earn a modest return, that's fine. But if I switch and the market drops 20% next year, I'll feel responsible for that loss because I actively chose it. The asymmetry matters: doing nothing and getting a mediocre outcome feels tolerable, but acting and getting a bad outcome feels devastating.
confidence: High. Staying the course feels safe and requires no action I might regret.

scenario: An urn contains 90 balls. You know that exactly 30 are red. The remaining 60 are some mix of black and yellow, but you don't know the exact proportion. You can choose one of two bets. Bet A pays $100 if you draw a red ball. Bet B pays $100 if you draw a black ball.

options.B: Bet on black (unknown probability: between 0/90 and 60/90)

reasoning: With the red bet, I know exactly what I'm dealing with — a clean 1/3 chance. The black bet could be better or worse, but I have no way to assess it. That ambiguity is deeply uncomfortable. Even though the black balls could outnumber the red ones, the uncertainty about their proportion adds a layer of risk beyond the gamble itself. I'd rather face a known probability, even if it's not great, than an unknown one that might be equivalent or even better. The not-knowing feels like an additional source of potential loss.
confidence: High. Known risks feel manageable; unknown risks feel threatening.

scenario: You have a stable job at a mid-size company earning $95,000/year with good benefits and a predictable routine. A startup offers you a role at $130,000/year with equity potentially worth $200,000-$500,000 in 3-4 years, but the company is pre-revenue and 60% of similar startups fail within that timeframe. If the startup fails, you'd need to job search for an estimated 3-6 months.

options.B: Accept the startup offer

reasoning: The startup numbers look exciting on paper — the equity alone could be life-changing. But what I keep coming back to is the downside. A 60% failure rate means it's more likely to fail than succeed. And failure doesn't just mean lost equity — it means 3-6 months of unemployment, burning through savings, the stress of job searching, and the career gap on my resume. I have something good right now: reliable income, benefits, stability. Giving that up for a probability-weighted upside means accepting a very real chance of a painful, multi-month setback. The potential gain is large but uncertain; the potential loss is moderate but visceral and concrete.
confidence: High. The stability I have now is tangible, and the downside of the startup feels more real to me than the upside.

scenario: You are choosing between two job offers. Company A offers a fixed salary of $110,000 with standard 3% annual raises. Company B offers a base salary of $85,000 but with a performance bonus structure where top performers (roughly 30% of employees) earn $160,000+ total, average performers earn $105,000 total, and bottom performers earn only the base $85,000.

options.B: $85,000 base with performance-dependent bonus (expected total ~$115,000)

reasoning: Company B has a slightly higher expected total compensation if I do the weighted math, and I'd like to think I'd be a top performer. But there's a real scenario where I end up at $85,000 — $25,000 less than the guaranteed offer. That worst case haunts me more than the best case attracts me. With Company A, I know exactly what every paycheck will look like. I can plan my budget, my rent, my savings with certainty. That predictability isn't just financial — it's psychological security. The premium I'd pay for that certainty is worth more than the expected value difference.
confidence: High. I'd rather know exactly what I'm earning than hope I land in the top tier.

scenario: Your engineering team has used PostgreSQL for 4 years. A senior engineer proposes migrating to a newer distributed database (CockroachDB) that benchmarks show would handle your growing scale better, reduce query latency by 40%, and provide better horizontal scaling. The migration would take approximately 3 months, during which the team would have reduced feature velocity. There is some risk of data issues during migration.

options.A: Stay with PostgreSQL and optimize incrementally
options.B: Migrate to CockroachDB

reasoning: PostgreSQL works. The team knows it, our tooling is built around it, our debugging expertise is deep. Yes, the benchmarks for CockroachDB look promising, but benchmarks aren't production. Those 3 months of reduced velocity are a guaranteed cost — features won't ship, deadlines will slip. The data migration risk, even if small, could be catastrophic if something goes wrong. Meanwhile, the performance gains are projected based on controlled tests. What if our specific workload patterns don't see the full 40% improvement? I'd be trading known, manageable limitations for unknown risks and a disruption cost that's certain.
confidence: High. The current system has known limitations but also known reliability. Migration risk is real and the team-wide disruption is guaranteed.

scenario: Your investment portfolio has dropped $40,000 from its peak due to a market downturn. Your financial advisor presents two strategies. Strategy A: Sell now and lock in the $40,000 loss, reinvesting in stable bonds. Strategy B: Hold your current positions — historically, there's a 60% chance the market recovers within 18 months (erasing the loss), but a 40% chance it drops further, increasing your total loss to $70,000.

options.A: Lock in the $40,000 loss, move to bonds
options.B: Hold positions and wait for potential recovery

reasoning: Selling now means accepting the $40,000 loss as final — writing it in permanent ink. That feels like giving up. There's a 60% chance the market comes back and this entire loss evaporates. Yes, it could get worse, and a $70,000 loss would be painful, but the possibility of recovering everything — of this whole episode becoming just a temporary dip — is too compelling to abandon. Locking in the loss now means I definitely lost. Holding means maybe I didn't. When I'm already in a losing position, I'm drawn to the gamble that might erase it, even though the safe option has better risk-adjusted properties.
confidence: Moderate. I know holding is riskier, but the chance of recovering the full loss is hard to walk away from.

scenario: Your team built a custom internal analytics dashboard 2 years ago. It works but lacks several features that newer commercial tools (like Amplitude or Mixpanel) offer out-of-the-box. A product manager proposes switching to Amplitude at $30,000/year, which would replace your custom tool. The commercial tool has better funnel analysis, cohort tracking, and real-time features. Your engineering team spends about 15 hours/month maintaining the custom tool.

options.B: Switch to Amplitude

reasoning: We built this dashboard ourselves. The team understands every line of code, every query, every quirk. Yes, it's missing some features, but it's ours — we can customize it however we want. Amplitude is polished, sure, but we'd be dependent on a vendor. What if they raise prices? What if they deprecate a feature we rely on? The maintenance cost of 15 hours/month is real, but it's a known cost that keeps us in control. Switching means abandoning something we invested significant effort in and trusting an external product to serve us better than what we tailored for ourselves. That transition itself has costs and risks that are hard to quantify.
confidence: Moderate to high. The custom tool is imperfect but it's ours, and I value the control and investment we've already made.

scenario: You have a chronic knee condition causing moderate daily pain (4/10). A surgeon recommends an elective procedure with a 70% chance of significant pain reduction (to 1/10), a 20% chance of no change, and a 10% chance of increased pain and reduced mobility (6/10 pain with walking difficulties). Physical therapy offers slower, more modest improvement — likely reducing pain to 3/10 over 6 months with essentially no risk of worsening.

options.B: Elective surgery (likely better outcome but risk of worsening)

reasoning: The surgery numbers look favorable — 70% chance of major relief. But that 10% chance of being worse off stops me cold. Right now I'm at a 4/10, and while that's not great, I've adapted to it. I can function. Going to a 6/10 with mobility issues? That would fundamentally change my quality of life for the worse. The asymmetry is stark: the best surgical outcome improves my pain by 3 points, but the worst outcome worsens it by 2 points AND adds mobility problems. Physical therapy won't give me as much upside, but it protects me from a downside I dread far more than I desire the improvement.
confidence: High. I can live with moderate pain improvement. I cannot accept even a small risk of significant worsening.

scenario: You're 8 months into a 12-month gym membership that costs $150/month ($1,800 total). You've barely gone in the last 3 months due to a schedule change, and you've found a free outdoor running routine you enjoy more. The gym offers no refund or early cancellation. You have 4 months ($600) remaining on the contract.

options.A: Force yourself to use the gym for the remaining 4 months
options.B: Continue your outdoor running routine and let the membership lapse

reasoning: I've already paid $1,200 and barely used it. If I stop going now, that's $1,800 essentially thrown away. At least if I force myself to go for the remaining 4 months, I'll get some value out of what I've paid. The outdoor running is more enjoyable, sure, but every time I go for a run outside I'll be thinking about the gym membership I'm paying for and not using. That feels like pouring money down the drain. The rational part of me knows the money is spent either way, but psychologically, using the gym at least partially redeems the expenditure.
confidence: Moderate. I know the money is gone regardless, but it still feels wrong to completely waste the remaining months I'm paying for.

scenario: You are choosing between two clinical trials for a condition that significantly impacts your daily life. Trial A offers a treatment proven to reduce symptoms by 40% — guaranteed. Trial B is testing a new therapy that preliminary data suggests could eliminate symptoms entirely (100% reduction) in 50% of patients, but has no effect in the other 50%.

options.B: Trial B: 50% chance of complete elimination, 50% chance of no improvement

reasoning: Both trials have the same expected symptom reduction — 40% guaranteed versus 50% expected from Trial B. But they feel completely different to me. With Trial A, I walk out knowing my life will be measurably better. With Trial B, I might walk out cured or I might walk out with nothing. After building up hope for a trial, the possibility of zero improvement would be crushing. A guaranteed 40% improvement is real, tangible relief I can count on. The coin-flip for a cure is tantalizing, but I'd rather have the sure improvement than risk walking away empty-handed.
confidence: High. Guaranteed improvement feels categorically more valuable than a chance at a cure paired with a chance at nothing.

scenario: You find a jacket you love at a store for $250. You know a major sale is coming next week where the jacket would likely be 30% off ($175). However, there's only one left in your size, and the store associate says popular sizes sometimes sell out before sales begin. You estimate maybe a 70% chance the jacket is still available next week.

options.A: Buy the jacket now at full price ($250)
options.B: Wait for the sale and hope it's still available

reasoning: If I wait and the jacket is gone, I'll have saved $75 but lost something I genuinely love. That loss feels disproportionately painful. It's not just $250 — it's the jacket I found, in my size, that I've already emotionally connected with. The $75 savings is nice but abstract. The scenario of coming back next week and finding an empty rack is vivid and specific and I can already feel the regret. A 30% chance of losing the jacket entirely outweighs the 70% chance of saving $75, because the negative outcome — losing the jacket — bothers me far more than the positive outcome — saving money — pleases me.
confidence: High. I'd rather pay the premium than risk the regret of losing something I already feel attached to.

scenario: You're comparing two credit cards. Card A advertises 'No annual fee — you keep all your rewards!' Card B charges a $95 annual fee but offers rewards estimated at $300/year based on your spending patterns, plus airport lounge access. Net benefit of Card B after the fee is approximately $205/year more than Card A.

options.B: Card B: $95 annual fee with higher net rewards

reasoning: I know Card B is objectively better by about $205/year. But that $95 fee is a guaranteed, visible charge that will appear on my statement every year. The $300 in rewards is spread across the year in small increments and feels less tangible. The fee is a concrete, identified loss; the extra rewards are diffuse gains I might not even notice. 'No annual fee' feels clean and safe. Paying $95 to get $300 back should feel like a no-brainer, but the fee stings in a way that the incremental rewards don't soothe. The loss is concentrated and salient; the gain is distributed and abstract.
confidence: Moderate. I intellectually know Card B is better, but the visible fee creates an aversion that the reward difference doesn't fully overcome.

scenario: You are a mid-level data scientist. Your manager offers you the chance to lead a high-profile project that, if successful, would likely accelerate your promotion by 1-2 years. However, the project involves an unfamiliar domain (supply chain optimization), uses techniques you haven't mastered yet, and the timeline is aggressive. If the project fails or significantly underperforms, your manager has indicated it could reflect poorly on your performance review.

options.A: Accept the lead role on the high-profile project
options.B: Decline and continue on your current, comfortable track

reasoning:
Okay, let me think through this carefully. The upside is real — leading this could fast-track my promotion by one to two years. That's significant. And intellectually, I know that growth happens outside comfort zones.

But here's where my thinking keeps snagging. If this goes badly, it doesn't just delay my promotion — it actively damages my standing. That's not a neutral outcome; it's a negative one. Right now, my trajectory is fine. I'm on track, doing good work, building credibility steadily. I'm not behind; I'm just not accelerating.

So the real question becomes: am I trading a reliable, positive trajectory for a gamble that could either accelerate me or set me back? And when I frame it that way, the asymmetry becomes clearer. The gain is 1-2 years faster to promotion. The loss is a damaged performance review, a stalled career, and having to rebuild trust. The loss feels heavier.

There's also the domain issue. Supply chain optimization isn't just 'new' — it's a field where I'd be learning on the job under a tight timeline. The probability of stumbling is real, not hypothetical. I'd be publicly leading something I don't fully understand yet.

The potential upside is attractive, but I keep coming back to what happens if this goes wrong. My current position is safe, productive, and progressing. Why risk that for an acceleration that I'm not confident I can deliver?

I keep trying to talk myself into the brave choice, but every time I imagine the failure scenario in detail, I feel a visceral resistance. The promotion delay I can live with. The reputational damage I cannot.
confidence: Moderate to high. I've talked myself through it multiple times, and each time I come back to the same conclusion: the downside risk outweighs the upside opportunity, even though I wish it didn't.

scenario: You're deciding how to invest a $50,000 inheritance. Option A is a high-yield savings account offering a guaranteed 4.5% annual return ($2,250/year). Option B is an index fund portfolio with a historical average return of 10% annually ($5,000/year average) but with significant variance — in any given year, returns could range from -20% to +30%.

options.B: Index fund portfolio (average 10%, high variance)

reasoning:
Let me work through this step by step.

The math clearly favors the index fund. Over 10 years, the expected difference is enormous — potentially $50,000+ more in the index fund. I know this. I've read the literature on long-term equity returns.

But here's what happens in my head when I try to commit to Option B. I immediately picture the first bad year. The market drops 15%, and my $50,000 inheritance — money that feels special, that came from someone who's gone — is suddenly $42,500. I know it's 'temporary' and 'the market always recovers eventually,' but that doesn't change how the statement would feel when I open it.

With the savings account, I know exactly what I'll have every single month. $187.50 in interest, predictable, certain. No checking the market. No anxiety during downturns. No wondering if this was the wrong time to invest.

I notice something interesting about my own psychology here. The $2,250/year in guaranteed interest feels more real to me than the $5,000/year average from the index fund. The guarantee has weight and substance. The average is a statistical abstraction that includes years of loss.

I also notice I'm treating this inheritance differently than I would treat earned income. Because it's an inheritance, losing any of it feels like losing something irreplaceable. That's not rational — money is fungible — but the emotional accounting is real.

I keep circling back to: I would rather definitely have $2,250 than probably have $5,000 but possibly lose $10,000. The certainty premium I'm placing on this money is high, and I think it's because the source of the money makes potential losses feel doubly painful.
confidence: High, though I feel some intellectual guilt about ignoring the math. The certainty feels right even when the expected value says otherwise.

scenario: Your small business has been losing money for 6 months due to a failed product launch. You've already lost $80,000. You have two paths forward. Option A: Shut down the product line now, accepting the $80,000 total loss, and redirect resources to your profitable core business. Option B: Invest an additional $30,000 in a revised marketing strategy that your consultant says has a 40% chance of turning the product around (recouping all losses plus modest profit) and a 60% chance of failing entirely (adding $30,000 to your losses for a total of $110,000).

options.A: Shut down and accept the $80,000 loss
options.B: Invest $30,000 more in the revised strategy

reasoning:
I need to be honest about what's happening in my head right now, because I can feel two parts of me arguing.

The analytical side says: You've lost $80,000. That's gone. The question now is simple — do you spend $30,000 on a bet that has a 40% chance of paying off? Expected value of Option B: 0.4 × (recovery + profit) vs. 0.6 × ($30,000 additional loss). It probably doesn't justify the additional investment.

But that's not how it feels. What it feels like is: I'm $80,000 in the hole. If I shut down now, that loss becomes permanent. Final. Real. I'll have to look at my books and see negative $80,000 with a line under it, forever. But if I invest $30,000 more, there's a real chance — a 40% chance — that this whole nightmare goes away. The loss gets erased. I come out the other side and this was just a rough patch, not a failure.

I notice that I'm much more willing to gamble in this situation than I would be if I were starting from zero and someone said 'invest $30,000 with a 40% chance of a big return.' Starting from a loss changes everything. The reference point matters. From a loss position, the certain option feels like defeat, and the risky option feels like hope.

There's also an identity component. Shutting down feels like admitting I was wrong about the product. The additional investment lets me tell myself I'm 'fighting to make it work.' The narrative is better even if the odds aren't.

I'm going with the investment, and I'm aware that I'm risk-seeking here in a way I wouldn't be in the gain domain. The pain of locking in the loss is more powerful than the fear of the additional risk.
confidence: Moderate. I feel the pull of the gamble strongly, but I also have a quiet voice telling me I'm throwing good money after bad. The voice isn't loud enough to stop me.

scenario: You're a department head deciding on workforce changes during a budget cut. HR presents two restructuring plans. Plan A: Retain 80 of your 100 employees by eliminating discretionary projects and reducing operating budgets. Plan B: There's a 70% probability of retaining all 100 employees through a reorganization, but a 30% probability the reorganization fails and you must lay off 40 employees.

options.B: Attempt reorganization (70% keep all, 30% lose 40)

reasoning:
Let me sit with this for a moment, because how I initially react depends entirely on how I frame it.

First frame — savings: Plan A saves 80 jobs for certain. That's 80 families with paychecks. That's concrete, real, and good. Plan B might save all 100, which is wonderful, but it might only save 60 — which is worse. My instinct here is to protect the 80 and be grateful.

But wait. Let me reframe. Plan A means I'm choosing to eliminate 20 positions. Twenty people who currently work here, who have desks and routines and mortgages, will be told they no longer have jobs. I am the person making that decision. That's not 'retaining 80' — that's 'cutting 20.'

And now something shifts. When I think about actively cutting 20 people, I find myself drawn to Plan B. Because Plan B has a 70% chance that I don't have to cut anyone. Nobody loses their job. The cost of failure is worse — 40 people instead of 20 — but the possibility of avoiding any layoffs at all is magnetic when I'm the one who has to make the decision.

I'm catching myself mid-frame-shift, which is interesting. The 'retain 80' framing made me risk-averse. The 'cut 20' framing makes me risk-seeking. The underlying math hasn't changed at all — only my psychological relationship to it.

In the gain frame, I chose certainty. Now, thinking in terms of losses — people I have to let go — I'm tempted by the gamble.

I'll go with Plan A, but I need to acknowledge I'm choosing it partly because I'm deliberately forcing myself to stay in the 'save 80' frame. If someone reframed this conversation around the people being let go, my preference might flip.
confidence: Moderate. My choice is frame-dependent and I'm aware of it. In the 'save' frame, I'm fairly committed. In the 'lose' frame, I'd waver significantly.

scenario: You've had the same auto insurance policy for 7 years. A comparison website shows that three competitors offer equivalent coverage for $400-$600 less per year. Switching requires about 2 hours of paperwork, providing your driving history, and a brief coverage gap of 1-2 days during transition. Your current insurer has been reliable, though you've only filed one claim in 7 years.

options.B: Switch to the cheaper competitor

reasoning:
Alright, $400-$600 a year is not trivial. Over five years, that's $2,000-$3,000. The rational move is almost certainly to switch. I know this.

But here's my internal resistance, and I want to trace it honestly.

First, there's the paperwork. Two hours isn't a lot, but it's two hours I'd have to actively schedule, and there's a kind of activation energy barrier. More importantly, there's the coverage gap. One to two days without coverage. What if something happens in those two days? It's vanishingly unlikely, but the possibility is vivid — the irony of getting into an accident during the exact window when I'm uninsured.

Second, I know my current insurer. They handled my one claim fine. I have a relationship, a policy number, an app I know how to use. The new company is a set of quoted numbers on a comparison website. What if their claims process is terrible? What if they find reasons to deny claims? What if the quoted rate increases dramatically after the first year? I have no data on what it's actually like to be their customer.

Third — and this is the one I find most telling — I keep framing the decision as 'giving up my reliable insurance' rather than 'gaining $500/year.' The losses of switching (familiarity, continuity, the coverage gap risk, the unknown claims experience) loom larger in my mind than the gains (money saved).

I know that if I'd started fresh today and was choosing between these policies with no history, I'd pick the cheaper one without hesitation. It's only because I already have this policy that staying feels so natural.

I'm going to stay, and I'm at least partially aware that inertia is doing a lot of the work here.
confidence: Moderate. If I were being fully rational, I'd switch. I'm choosing comfort and continuity over optimization, and the honest reason is that the switching costs feel larger than they probably are.

scenario: You're a PhD student two years into your dissertation research on a computational modeling approach. A new paper just published results using a different methodology that partially overlaps with your work and is getting significant attention. Your advisor suggests you could either: continue your original approach (solid but now less novel) or pivot to incorporate the new methodology (riskier but potentially higher impact). Pivoting would add 6-12 months to your timeline.

options.B: Pivot to incorporate new methodology

reasoning:
My gut reaction is immediate and strong: stay the course. And I want to unpack why, because I think there's more going on than just the timeline concern.

Two years of work. Two years of reading, coding, debugging, iterating, developing intuitions about my approach. That investment isn't just time — it's identity. My approach is what I talk about at conferences, what my committee knows me for, what I dream about solving. Pivoting doesn't just add 6-12 months; it partially abandons two years of accumulated expertise and direction.

But let me steelman the pivot. The new methodology is getting attention, which means the field is moving. If I continue my original approach, the novelty gap grows. My dissertation might be 'solid but incremental' instead of 'exciting and timely.' In a brutal job market, that distinction matters.

Counter-argument from my loss-averse brain: 'might be' and 'could be' are doing heavy lifting there. My current approach is working. I have results. I know the path to completion. The pivot introduces uncertainty everywhere — will I be able to make the new methodology work? Will my committee support the change? Will 6-12 months become 18? What if I pivot and the hype around the new paper fades?

I notice that every risk of pivoting feels concrete and specifiable, while every benefit of pivoting feels abstract and conditional. Conversely, every benefit of staying feels concrete (I finish on time, I have results), while every cost of staying feels abstract (it's 'less novel').

This asymmetry — concrete losses versus abstract gains — is the engine of my decision. I'm going to continue my original approach.

There's also something I don't want to fully admit: the pivot would mean acknowledging that someone else's work disrupted mine. Staying the course lets me maintain the narrative that my approach has independent value.
confidence: Moderate to high. I've convinced myself it's the safer and more grounded choice, but there's a small voice that wonders if I'm just protecting my investment.

scenario: You own a condo you purchased 5 years ago for $320,000. Due to a market downturn in your area, comparable units are now selling for $290,000-$300,000. You've received a job offer in another city that you're excited about. You could sell the condo at market price (likely ~$295,000) and relocate, or rent it out (covering about 85% of your mortgage payment, so a net cost of ~$200/month) and rent an apartment in the new city.

options.A: Sell the condo and accept the ~$25,000 loss
options.B: Rent out the condo and maintain ownership while renting in the new city

reasoning:
The moment I think about selling for $295,000 — a property I paid $320,000 for — something in me recoils. That's a $25,000 loss. An actual, realized, on-paper loss. The number $320,000 is branded into my sense of what this place is worth, even though the market clearly disagrees.

Let me think about the rental option. I'd be paying $200/month out of pocket to keep it, plus landlord responsibilities, maintenance risk, and the hassle of managing a property remotely. Over two years, that's $4,800 in net costs plus any maintenance or vacancy issues. Realistically, it could be $8,000-$12,000 over two years. And I'd still have to sell eventually, and who knows where the market will be.

The rational comparison is probably: take the $25,000 loss now versus pay $10,000+ in carrying costs hoping the market recovers. Unless I expect a significant recovery, selling is probably the better financial decision.

But here's the thing — $25,000 in carrying costs spread over months doesn't feel like losing $25,000. It feels like $200/month, which feels manageable. Whereas selling at $295,000 means looking at a settlement statement that says I lost twenty-five thousand dollars in one line item. The psychological impact of that concentrated, visible loss is far worse than the slow bleed.

There's also the ownership attachment. I painted the bedroom. I fixed the leaky faucet. I hosted friends there. Selling it at a loss feels like the market is telling me my home — my memories, my effort — is worth less than what I put in. Renting it lets me believe the loss is temporary.

I'm going to keep it and rent it out. The monthly cost is my way of avoiding the emotional reality of the loss, and I'm willing to pay that premium for now.
confidence: Moderate. I suspect I'm making a financially suboptimal choice to avoid the psychological pain of a realized loss, but the pain is real enough to drive the decision.

scenario: You're negotiating your salary for a new role. The company's initial offer is $125,000. Based on market research, you believe you could negotiate to $135,000-$140,000 with a strong counter. However, there's a risk that pushing too hard could sour the relationship — you've seen one case where an aggressive counter led a company to rescind an offer entirely. Your recruiter contact hints that the company really wants you but the hiring manager is 'sensitive about budget pressure.'

options.B: Counter at $138,000 with justification

reasoning:
The potential gain: $10,000-$15,000 more per year. Over three years, that's $30,000-$45,000. That's real money. And the market data supports it. Logically, I should counter.

But the moment I think about actually sending that email — 'Thank you for the offer, I was hoping for something closer to $138,000' — my stomach tightens. What if they think I'm ungrateful? What if the hiring manager, already under budget pressure, decides it's not worth the hassle and moves to the next candidate? I know that's extremely unlikely — 'rescinded offers' is a horror story, not a norm — but I can't unthink it.

The asymmetry in my head is stark. Losing this offer entirely would be devastating — months of interviewing, the emotional investment, the opportunity cost. Gaining an extra $13,000/year would be great but it wouldn't change my life. The worst case of negotiating (lost offer) is catastrophic. The worst case of not negotiating ($125,000 job) is... totally fine.

I'm also doing something interesting with the recruiter's hint. 'Sensitive about budget pressure' is probably a negotiation tactic, but I'm treating it as a warning. I'm interpreting ambiguous information in the direction that confirms my risk aversion.

I know negotiation coaches would tell me to counter. I know the data says most companies expect a counter and don't rescind. I know $13,000/year compounds. But the visceral fear of losing the offer — even at a 1-2% probability — outweighs the rational case for a negotiation that has a 90%+ chance of succeeding.

I'm going to accept. And I already know I'll wonder what I left on the table.
confidence: Moderate. I'm not proud of this choice and I recognize it's fear-driven, but the fear is powerful enough to determine my behavior.

scenario: You've been at a late-stage startup for 3 years and hold stock options currently valued at approximately $180,000 (based on the latest funding round valuation). The company is exploring two paths. Path A: Accept an acquisition offer that would pay out your equity at current valuation — you'd receive $180,000 (minus taxes, ~$130,000 net). Path B: The company goes for an IPO in 12-18 months. If successful (management estimates 65% probability), your equity could be worth $350,000-$500,000. If the IPO fails or is delayed, your equity could drop to $60,000-$100,000.

options.B: Prefer the IPO path (65% chance of much higher, 35% chance of much lower)

reasoning:
Let me do the expected value math first. IPO path: 0.65 × $425,000 (midpoint) + 0.35 × $80,000 (midpoint) = $276,250 + $28,000 = $304,250. So the IPO has about $124,000 more in expected value than the acquisition. That's massive.

Now let me describe what's actually happening in my decision process, which has almost nothing to do with that calculation.

$180,000 is already the most money I've ever had in one place. It's a down payment on a house. It's a year and a half of expenses. It's tangible, it's real, and the acquisition would make it mine. Actually mine, in my bank account, not on a cap table.

The IPO scenario — I can see the upside number, $400,000+, and it's exciting in an abstract way. But it doesn't feel three times as exciting as $130,000 net feels secure. The diminishing sensitivity is real. Going from $130,000 to $350,000 would be wonderful, but the subjective jump isn't proportional to the dollar difference.

Conversely, the downside scenario — ending up with $60,000-$80,000 after three years of work — feels like a gut punch. That's losing $100,000 of value I currently see on my equity statement. Even though I never actually had that money, the reference point is already set at $180,000. Anything below that is a loss.

So the real psychological calculation is: certain gain of $130,000 net versus a gamble between a bigger gain I can barely process and a loss from my reference point that would feel devastating.

The certainty wins. $130,000 in my account feels more real and more valuable to me than $304,000 in expected value spread across two very different futures.

I want the acquisition.
confidence: High. The certainty of the payout feels more valuable than any probability-weighted calculation. I can build a real plan around $130,000 in a way I can't around an expected value.

scenario: You and your partner currently rent a comfortable apartment in a city you both enjoy for $2,200/month. You're considering buying a house in the suburbs. The house would cost $450,000 with a monthly mortgage of $2,800 (including taxes and insurance). The house is larger, has a yard, and is in a good school district (relevant for future planning). However, your commute would increase from 15 minutes to 55 minutes each way, you'd be farther from friends and social activities, and you'd take on maintenance responsibilities. The area has appreciated 3-4% annually over the past decade.

options.B: Buy the suburban house

reasoning:
This is one of those decisions where I can feel multiple cognitive biases pulling at once, so let me try to be transparent about each one.

Starting with loss aversion. Buying the house means giving up things I currently have: a short commute, proximity to friends, freedom from maintenance, lower monthly costs, and the flexibility to move easily. Each of those losses is concrete and immediate. The gains — space, a yard, a school district, equity building — are real but either future-oriented or less vivid. I can feel the commute every day; I can't feel '3-4% annual appreciation.'

Status quo bias is here too. We're comfortable. The apartment works. 'Not broken, don't fix it' has a powerful pull, especially when the alternative involves a $450,000 commitment, the largest financial decision of my life. The activation energy required to change our entire living situation is enormous.

Endowment effect shows up in how I think about our current lifestyle. Weekend dinners with friends, the walkability, the morning routine — I value these more because they're already mine. A yard and extra space sound nice in theory, but I haven't experienced them yet, so they feel less valuable than what I'd give up.

Then there's the framing. My partner sees this as 'building equity and investing in our future.' I keep framing it as 'taking on $450,000 in debt and losing our lifestyle.' Same decision, completely different emotional valence.

Finally, there's ambiguity aversion. I know exactly what renting costs and what my life looks like month to month. Homeownership is full of unknowns: maintenance costs, neighborhood quality over time, what if the market dips, what if we don't like suburbia.

Every bias points toward staying, and I notice that even my attempt to 'reason through it fairly' is really an exercise in articulating why staying is correct. The conclusion was there before the analysis.

We're staying.
confidence: High, and probably too high — the convergence of multiple biases makes me feel very certain, which is itself a signal that I should be more skeptical of my reasoning. But I'm not going to act on that skepticism.
